| Mirin | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |

Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients  | Alcoholic Drink

**Mirin** is a sweet Japanese rice-based wine, commonly used in Japanese cooking.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4) It is not the same thing as sake.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

Mirin is produced by combining cooked glutinous rice, koji mold, and shochu (a liquor made from rice wine).[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5) The mold converts the rice starch to glucose and various aromatics, while the shochu prevents the production of excess alcohol, retaining the sweetness.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5) Finally, it is filtered and clarified.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)

The final product has an alcohol content of about 14% (lower than that of sake),[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6) as well as a high sugar content and sweetness.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) The flavor is described as rich and layered,[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5) and the color is light golden.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)

### Varieties

[edit | edit source]

Products marketed as mirin are typically available in a few varieties. **Hon mirin** ("true mirin") is a higher grade made using the production process described above,[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8) meaning they are aged and have the full alcohol content and complex flavor.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2) This style should generally contain only rice, the koji, shochu, and perhaps a small amount of salt, with no added sugar.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2) **Shio mirin** ("salt mirin") is similar, but with added salt such that it is not drinkable as a beverage in itself.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)

Because of various restrictions on alcoholic beverages, as well as the higher price tag of hon mirins, a number of mirin approximants are produced.[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) **Aji-mirin** or **mirin-style condiment/seasoning** is made with alcohol (~8%), starch or glucose syrup, rice, and added salt.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8) **Mirin-like condiments**, sometimes called **kotteri mirin**, contain little to no alcohol, approximating the flavor of mirin with starch syrups and rice seasonings.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4) This variety has lower complexity of flavor and is very sweet.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)

Note that the labeling can vary somewhat for all these products, so checking the ingredients is the best way to know what type of product you are getting.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Given the different varieties, the cook will need to decide which grade of mirin is suitable for their purpose. Lower grades, such as mirin-like condiments, may be adequate for some dishes, while other preparations may need higher-grade mirins.[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)

The best place to look for mirin is grocery stores catering to east Asian (and especially Japanese) cuisines,[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4) though unsalted, alcoholic varieties may be harder to source, depending on the regulations in a given region.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2) Check the ingredient list to help confirm which variety of mirin it is.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)

Hon mirin, containing alcohol, can generally be stably stored in the dark at cool room temperature for several months; it may darken as it ages.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) You can store it in the fridge if desired,[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8) but the sugar may crystallize.[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4) No- or low-alcohol varieties should be stored in the refrigerator after opening or according to the manufacturer directions.[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Mirin's primary role in cooking is to complement the final dish using its alcohol, sweetness, and the complex flavor from fermentation.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5) It also adds a characteristic glossiness.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5) One very well-known application for mirin is teriyaki sauce,[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6) which is one of several sauces made with it.[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7)

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

The general recommendation for achieving results with the best flavor is to buy the highest-quality mirin you can find and afford—aim for hon mirin when possible.[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5) If you can't get a higher grade, you can try a lower grade (e.g. aji mirin) or try one of several substitutions.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5)[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8) If you can get a different rice wine like sake or Chinese Shaoxing, try dissolving sugar in the wine at a 3:1 volume ratio of wine to sugar in order to sweeten it.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) You can also try a white wine, sherry, or marsala,[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) but the flavors will be different due to the use of grapes.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)
